title: RBA bracing for private credit default rates to increase
full_text:
The Reserve Bank of Australia is bracing for mounting defaults across the ballooning private credit sector and is concerned that any financial shock from the industry will be exacerbated by highly concentrated loan books in the property sector among fund managers.
Internal documents from the central bank released to The Australian Financial Review under Freedom of Information also reveal that the RBA is worried about the fallout from a rupture in overseas private credit markets, such as the collapse of a major fund.
“There are concerns that a severe stress event affecting large offshore private credit providers could have spillover effects in Australia,” said the RBA in an internal review of non-bank lenders that was conducted in December.
“International players with deep funding capacity could also enter the Australian market quickly, significantly increasing domestic growth and amplifying the risks of global spillover.”
The RBA joins the European Central Bank and the Bank of England, which have also conducted reviews into the growing risks that private credit poses to global financial stability. The Bank of International Settlements last month warned of visible signs of stress in private credit.
The RBA flagged the heavy exposure of local private credit funds to real estate and construction.
“The Australian Securities and Investments Commission reports material sectoral concentration in development and construction projects, which can amplify financial shocks in an adverse scenario to the real estate industry”, the central bank said in its internal review.
It warned that such shocks could flow from a “housing price crash, or sustained increase in construction costs”, which are notable concerns for the Australian economy as house prices decline and home building costs rise.
In addition to the RBA’s December review of private credit, the FOI release included a memo on the global private credit sector that was prepared by a senior analyst in the central bank’s financial stability department in May.
“Default rates are rising and are projected to structural [sic] increase over the rest of 2026,” the memo read.
The analyst noted that banks had “increasingly provided leverage to private credit funds through the use of lending facilities” and estimated the value of these loan commitments stretched into the hundreds of billions of dollars.
Heavy offshore exposure
The analyst did note, however, that compared to banks, private credit funds typically employed less leverage and were protected from sudden mass withdrawals by redemption limits.
That meant growth in the private credit sector could theoretically have a “net positive effect” on financial stability, according to the memo. But the unnamed analyst also said it was “unclear whether this [benefit] holds under extreme stress scenarios”.
Private credit funds have proliferated in the years following the global financial crisis as banks pulled back from the lending market. They have become increasingly popular, particularly among retail investors, on the promise of high returns.
But a spate of high-profile redemption “gatings” – where the funds refuse investor requests to withdraw money – including from US private credit giants Ares and Apollo, has tested the market’s nerve.
At the same time, concern is growing over the exposure of offshore private credit funds to artificial intelligence and software-related firms that were heavily sold off earlier in the year.
The RBA’s December review noted that the Australian private credit market had “grown significantly over the past decade” with total assets under management possibly as high as $250 billion, up from $35 billion in 2014.
Analysis commissioned by the RBA’s assistant governor Brad Jones – that was contained in the set of emails released under FOI – revealed an estimated $150 billion, or 92 per cent, of Australian bank exposure to non-bank financial institution lending came from overseas.
The analysis also estimated that Australian bank exposure to local private credit funds could be as high as $12 billion.
That number ballooned to $162 billion once all non-resident non-bank lending exposures were taken into account, which amounts to about 3 per cent of the total assets of Australian banks.
The RBA is worried about global private credit spending patterns, noting that about half of the artificial intelligence infrastructure investment from the hyperscalers was expected to be funded by debt.
“Deeper links between AI firms and credit markets … mean that should an asset price correction occur, losses on lending could increase financial stability risks,” the RBA said in commentary on financial markets in March.
It added that the opacity of debt arrangements in sectors like private credit “could lead to unforeseen losses under stressed conditions”.
The RBA review also noted that opaque investment structures deployed by private credit firms limited both regulatory and investor understanding of the sector.
“As private credit in Australia expands, the sector’s opacity limits regulators’ ability to assess financial stability vulnerabilities,” it said.
“Such opacity could lead investors to mis-assess, mis-price or mis-hedge the risks they are taking, in turn amplifying risks spreading through the system in an adverse scenario.”

heading: Banking and Economy
